title: Why the End of the Financial Year Is the Beginning of Your Capital Plan
linkedin:
June 30 is four days away. Most businesses are using it to close the year. The sharper ones are using it to plan the next.

The compliance work is essential. Accounts get finalised, returns get lodged, BAS gets submitted. That all happens regardless.

What tends not to happen alongside it is the capital conversation. The same data going to the accountant also contains the clearest picture you will have all year of how your business actually funds itself.

Three numbers are worth pulling from the completed accounts before July 1 arrives.

The first is cash generated from operations. Not profit. The actual cash your business produced from its trading activity across the twelve months.

The second is the change in your net working capital position. How much cash did the year's growth consume? If revenue grew but working capital grew faster, that gap had to come from somewhere.

The third is peak borrowing versus year-end borrowing. What was the most your business drew on its facilities during the year? The difference between that peak and where you sit on June 30 tells you whether your facilities were actually sized for the year you ran.

A lot of facilities were set up two or three years ago. Revenue has grown, the working capital cycle has changed, and the covenants may be tighter or looser than they should be.

Banks assess new and increased facilities against your most recent completed accounts. The strongest application window opens immediately after EOFY. If you need a facility in place by September, the work starts now.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog. Link in the first comment.

facebook:
With June 30 arriving this week, most business owners are focused on getting the accounts finalised and the compliance work across the line. Fair enough.

But those same completed accounts tell you something important about how to fund the year ahead: whether your facilities are still sized right, whether you are generating enough cash to fund your own growth, and whether the instrument you are using still fits the business you are running now.

One question worth sitting with before July 1: does your current funding structure reflect the business you have today, or the one you had when you last reviewed it?

#AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
google:
With the end of the financial year arriving on June 30, most business owners across Brisbane, Queensland, and the East Coast are focused on finalising their accounts and getting compliance work across the line. That is exactly the right priority.

What tends to happen less often is a structured look at what those completed accounts are telling you about your capital position heading into FY27.

The full year's accounts surface three things that monthly reports rarely make visible: the actual cash your business generated from operations, how much of that cash the year's growth consumed in working capital, and whether your banking facilities were sized for the year you actually ran.

The window to act on that picture opens now. Banks assess new and increased facilities against completed annual accounts, and the strongest application period is immediately after EOFY when the data is fresh. If you need a facility in place by September or October, the structuring and application work needs to start in the next few weeks.

The new financial year is a natural reset point. Using it to reassess your funding structure, not just close off the old year, puts your business in a stronger position to act when growth opportunities arrive.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
